text: Comprehensive metabolome analysis remains a challenge for the “omics” community due to the tremendous diversity and variability of small molecules existing in nature. Typically, untargeted LC/MS approaches favor the polar fraction due to the inherently larger elution time required for the separation of non-polar analytes on RPLC columns and preferential loss of non-polar species in the void volume when using HILIC columns. To address this issue, we here report an integrated dual-LC/MS method to bridge the gap of separation between the polar and non-polar fractions of the metabolome. After evaluating several aminopropyl HILIC columns and gradients, we found that using a pH 9.8 gradient containing specific concentrations of ammonium ions as adducts resulted in the most favorable conditions for HILIC/MS separation of polar metabolites while maintaining excellent chromatographic repeatability and column stability at extended run times ideal for detecting those metabolites involved in central carbon metabolism which are typically lost in the void volume. By using a single-extraction approach and combining data from paired RPLC and HILIC columns eluted in ESI positive and negative modes for the analyses of bacterial extracts, human plasma and cancer cells, we were able to identify over 30,000 features for each sample type. Our results demonstrated that the largest amount of biological information including lipid and metabolites involved in central carbon metabolism were identified by combining RPLC ESI(+) and HILIC ESI(-). Thus, the method reported here provides a reliable approach for increasing the coverage of the metabolome towards an “omics” level.
